summary:
The Pre-Pottery Neolithic B (PPNB) is an archaeological culture of the Levant and neighboring regions, dated approximately 8500–6500 BCE, representing the most fully developed phase of early Neolithic life in southwestern Asia before the introduction of pottery. It follows the [[event:1304|Pre-Pottery Neolithic A]] (PPNA) and is characterized by larger, more permanent settlements, more extensive agricultural and herding economies, a distinctive repertoire of stone tools including pressure-flaked projectile points, and a rich symbolic and ritual culture.

PPNB communities occupied a broad swathe of the Near East, from the southern Levant through Syria and into southeastern Anatolia and the upper Tigris and Euphrates basins. Major sites include [[event:7|Ain Ghazal]], Kfar Hahoresh, Basta, and multiple settlements in the Syrian steppe and Anatolian foothills. Settlement sizes grew considerably compared to PPNA, with some sites covering several hectares and housing hundreds of inhabitants in rectangular mud-brick houses arranged around communal spaces.

The PPNB is notable for a distinctive mortuary practice — the removal, defleshing, and separate burial of human skulls, sometimes with plastered and painted features — that was widespread across the culture zone. These plastered skulls, found at [[event:2|Jericho]], Ain Ghazal, Kfar Hahoresh, and other sites, are interpreted as evidence of ancestor veneration or the ritual preservation of the identities of deceased community members. The [[event:1323|skull cult practices]] of the PPNB represent one of the most elaborate known expressions of prehistoric ritual behavior in the ancient Near East.
